speaker: Interviewer
text: Vikram, thank you for showing us the shop floor. Aequs' scale in this single SEZ is impressive, but you mentioned earlier that the 'vertical integration' isn't as smooth as it looks on paper. Where are the actual friction points?

speaker: Vikram Singh
text: It's the raw material flow. Even though everything is in one zone, we're seeing major delays in the handoff between forging and precision machining. The 10,000-ton press is a beast, but if the downstream machining units aren't perfectly synced, the material just sits there. It's a massive vertical integration bottleneck.

speaker: Interviewer
text: Is that a scheduling problem, or a lack of visibility into the machining status?

speaker: Vikram Singh
text: It's both. We don't have a real-time 'digital twin' of the floor. Right now, I can't look at a screen and see exactly which machining centers are free and which are overloaded. We're relying on manual walk-throughs to manage the backlog. If we had a digital twin for machining, I could optimize the throughput in real-time.

speaker: Interviewer
text: And that scrap rate we saw earlier—is that related to this lack of synchronization?

speaker: Vikram Singh
text: Exactly. When the machines are rushed due to poor scheduling, the settings aren't always perfect, and we end up with more rework than I’m comfortable with. Reducing that scrap is my number one priority for lowering our unit cost.

speaker: Interviewer
text: High-fidelity visualization seems like a clear next step. We'll focus the next phase of the gap analysis on the data layer required for that digital twin.
